coredata.dc:title: Sensitive determination of kaempferol using carbon dots as a fluorescence probe
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lizhen
Feng, Feng
Chin Paau, Man
Hu, Qin
Liu, Yang
Chen, Zezhong
Bai, Yunfeng
Guo, Fangfang
Choi, Martin M.F.
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, a new sensitive and convenient method for the determination of kaempferol (Kae) based on the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) was developed. The C-dots were prepared by simply mixing acetic acid, water and diphosphorus pentoxide. This green synthesis approach proceeds rapidly and gives large quantities of C-dots. The fluorescence of the C-dots decreased obviously with the increase of Kae concentration. The effect of other interfering substances on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots showed low interference. Under the optimum conditions, a linear correlation was established between fluorescence intensity ratio F
o /F and the concentration of Kae in the range of 3.5–49µM with a detection limit (S/N=3) of 38.4nM. The proposed method has been successfully applied to determination of Kae in xindakang tablets and human serum samples with recovery in the range of 94.6–109.0%. The C-dots could be a promising fluorescence probe for the detection of Kae owing to its low-cost production, easy operation, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence probe and kaempferol

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pH and reaction time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the as-prepared C-dots. (B) The particle size distribution of C-dots is obtained by counting randomly 200 particles from various spots of the sample during TEM imaging.
MALDI-TOF mass spectrum of the as-prepared C-dots. The inset displays the enlarged mass spectrum of the largest mass ions.

(A) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots (1) in the absence and (2) the presence of Kae. (B) The relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity (F
o /F) and pH. 0.10mg/mL C-dots and 10.5µM Kae are used. Each data point is the average of three measurements. The error bars indicate the standard deviation of the measurements.

(A) Fluorescence responses of C-dots in the presence of different interfering substances. (B) Fluorescence response of C-dots in the mixed interfering substances with and without Kae. 0.10mg/mL C-dots and 35µM Kae are used. The concentration of interfering substances are 50µM for K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Glu, and Cys, 500µM for Thy, Gly, Ala, His, and Try, 100µM for Rut, Chr, Chl, Dai, Fer, Catl, Epi, and Catn. Each data point is the average of three measurements. The error bars indicate the standard deviation of the measurements.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots in the absence and presence of Kae, from top to bottom, the concentration of Kae are 0.0, 3.5, 7.0, 10.5, 14.0, 17.5, 21.0, 24.5, 28.0, 31.5, 35.0, 38.5, 42.0, 45.5, and 49.0µM. (B) The relationship between F
o /F and Kae concentration, where F
0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Kae. 0.10mg/mL C-dots is used. Each data point is the average of three measurements. The error bars indicate the standard deviation of the measurements.
Analytical results of Kae in real samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive determination of kaempferol using carbon dots as a fluorescence probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Partner State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis, and Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, 224 Waterloo Road, Kowloon Tong, Hong Kong SAR, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Partner State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis, and Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China. Fax: +86 352 6100028.
Corresponding author. Present address: Acadia Divinity College, Acadia University, 15 University Avenue, Wolfville, Nova Scotia, Canada B4P 2R6. Fax: +852 34117348.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Exchange student on visit to Hong Kong Baptist University.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, a new sensitive and convenient method for the determination of kaempferol (Kae) based on the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) was developed. The C-dots were prepared by simply mixing acetic acid, water and diphosphorus pentoxide. This green synthesis approach proceeds rapidly and gives large quantities of C-dots. The fluorescence of the C-dots decreased obviously with the increase of Kae concentration. The effect of other interfering substances on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots showed low interference. Under the optimum conditions, a linear correlation was established between fluorescence intensity ratio F
o /F and the concentration of Kae in the range of 3.5–49µM with a detection limit (S/N=3) of 38.4nM. The proposed method has been successfully applied to determination of Kae in xindakang tablets and human serum samples with recovery in the range of 94.6–109.0%. The C-dots could be a promising fluorescence probe for the detection of Kae owing to its low-cost production, easy operation, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Kaempferol (Kae) shown in Fig. S1 is a kind of common flavonoids which is abundant in tea, broccoli, apples, strawberries and beans [1]. During the past few years, Kae has drawn extensive attention as it displays a variety of biological activities including anticarcinogenic [2,3], antiatherogenic [4], antioxidant [5], antiphlogistic [6], and osteogenic properties [7]. It is commonly used for treating cough, ulcer, bronchial asthma, diabetes, cataract, and epilepsy and can be considered as an anti-inflammatory drug [8]. It also possesses cancer chemopreventive, neuroprotective, antidepressant and anxiolytic effects. The function of dilating coronary blood vessels makes it an effective drug for heart diseases [9]. However, over dose of Kae may cause some side effects. Thus it is necessary and essential to develop a sensitive detection method of Kae for controlling drug dosage and monitoring drug metabolism.

So far various analytical methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10–12], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [13], HPLC-MS [14], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [15,16], and micellar electrokinetic chromatography (MEKC) [17] have been developed for determination of Kae. These methods may provide good precision and high sensitivity, but have some disadvantages including operation complexity, time and material consumption, and expensive equipment. Compared with other analytical techniques, fluorescence methods are generally fast and economical in trace analysis. They have the advantages of simplicity and high sensitivity. To our knowledge, there is only one report on fluorescence detection of Kae based on thioglycollic acid-capped CdTe quantum dots as the fluorescence probe [18]. The linear range and the limit of detection were 4–44 and 0.79μg/mL, respectively. Therefore, developing a highly sensitive and selective method for determination of Kae would still be of considerable value.

Carbon-based materials possess a broad range of properties due to the existence of different allotropes as well as various microstructures. Carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes, carbon fibers, porous materials, fullerene, and graphene have drawn considerable attention owing to their excellent electrical and thermal conductivity, high strength and stability, good flexibility and low weight. C-dots are the latest form of carbon-based materials that are attracting increasing interest because they possess distinct benefits including chemical inertness, low photobleaching, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and environmentally friendly as compared with organic dyes and other semiconductor nanodots with heavy metal cores [19,20]. So a great deal of efforts has been focused on contriving better synthetic routes to produce C-dots with desirable performance. Recent advancements in this area include electrochemical oxidation [21,22], laser ablation [23,24], hydrothermal synthesis [25,26], pyrolysis [27,28], microwave-assisted heating [29–31], and self-catalysis [32]. Among them, self-catalysis is regarded as a simple, direct and efficient route for synthesis of C-dots with large quantities. This automatic synthesis is self-promoted and self-controlled by the chemical and physical properties of the reactants without any external treatment.

C-dots with tunable emission are considered to be the next generation of green nanomaterials and can be used in a wide range of technologies such as bioimaging [33,34], photocatalysis [35], lasers [36] and energy conversion/storage devices [37]. In addition, the applications of C-dots as fluorescence probes for various analytes have been studied by several researchers and the outcomes of their studies were promising. Zong et al. used C-dots as fluorescent probes for “off-on” detection of Cu2+ and ʟ-cysteine in aqueous solution [38]. Wee et al. used C-dots as a sensitive sensing probe for Pb2+ [39]. Zhang et al. used nitrogen-doped C-dots as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ [40]. Ahmed et al. used fluorescent C-dots for sensitive and selective detection of tannic acid in wines [41]. Qu et al. developed a C-dots-based fluorescent probe for Cu2+ [42] and Zhang et al. synthesized highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped C-dots for sensitive and selective probing Fe3+ in living cells [43]. However, to our knowledge, the application of C-dots as fluorescence probe for the quantitative determination of Kae has not been reported so far.

In this article, for the first time, we demonstrate the use of C-dots as a fluorescence probe for the determination of Kae. C-dots were prepared by simply mixing acetic acid, water and diphosphorus pentoxide. These C-dots can be served as an effective fluorescence probe for detection of Kae with high sensitivity and selectivity. The determination of kaempferol was based on its quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots. Finally, the proposed method has been successfully applied to determination of Kae in xindakang tablets and human serum samples with satisfactory results.

Diphosphorus pentoxide (P2O5) and 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB, 98%) were obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Kaempferol (Kae), ferulic acid (Fer), daidzein (Dai), chrysophanol (Chr), rutin (Rut), chlorogenic acid (Chl), catechin (Catn), l-epicatechin (Epi), and catechol (Catl) were bought from the National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biological Products (Beijing, China). Glacial acetic acid and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were from Fisher Chemicals (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA). Dibasic sodium phosphates (Na2HPO4·7H2O) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) were obtained from Mallinckrodt Chemical (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). Potassium bromide (KBr) was purchased from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was obtained from Acros Organics (Geel, Belgium). Methanol (MeOH) and acetonitrile (ACN) was from Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand). NaCl, KCl, PbCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, Zn(NO3)2, FeNO3, CaCl2, threonine (Thr), glycine (Gly), glutamic acid (Glu), alanine (Ala), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), and tryptophan (Try) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Xindakang tablets were obtained from Sichuan Xindakang Pharmaceutical Company Limited (Chengdu, China). Distilled deionized (DDI) water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q-RO4 water purification system with a resistivity higher than 18MΩcm−1 (Bedford, MA, USA). All reagents of analytical reagent grade or above were used as received without further purification.

The C-dots samples were synthesized according to a literature method with slight modifications [32]. In brief, a mixture of 1.0mL glacial acetic acid and 80µL DDI water was quickly added to 2.5g P2O5 in a 25mL beaker without stirring. The synthesis was carried out in a fumehood to prevent inhalation of acetic acid vapor. After the reaction, the beaker was cooled down within 10min to obtain the dark brown solid. The crude product was then dispersed in water and purified by dialysis with a Spectrum Laboratories cellulose ester dialysis membrane tube (molecular weight cut-off 1000Da) (Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) in DDI water with stirring and recharging with fresh DDI water every 24h for 7 days. Finally, the product from the dialysis membrane tube was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 10min to remove the supernatant. The dark brown deposits were collected and freeze-dried to obtain pure C-dots powder. A certain amount of the C-dots powder was dissolved in MeOH at the desired concentration for further use.

The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of the as-prepared C-dots was obtained from a Nicolet Magna 550 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, West Palm Beach, FL, USA) with the KBr pellet technique ranging 500–4000cm−1. The C-dots/KBr disk was obtained by mixing few milligrams of C-dots with ca. 100mg KBr powder, grinding to approximately 2µm in an agate mortar, and subjecting to 29.7MPa pressure by a pressing machine.

The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of the as-prepared C-dots were acquired on a Leybold Heraeus SKL-12 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shenyang, China). Spectra were processed by the Casa XPS v.2.3.12 software using a peak-fitting routine with symmetrical Gaussian-Lorentzian functions.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared C-dots was acquired on a Varian Cary 300 scan UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Palo Alto, CA, USA) at 200–700nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the as-prepared C-dots were determined on a Hitachi F-2500 spectrofluorometer equipped with a xenon discharge lamp and a 1.0cm-quartz cell (Hitachi High-Technologies Co., Tokyo, Japan). Φ
S of the as-prepared C-dots was determined by comparing the integrated PL intensities and absorbances of C-dots with quinine sulfate as the reference. The experimental details of Φ
S determination of the as-prepared C-dots are deposited in Supplementary data.

The mass spectrum (MS) of the as-prepared C-dots was obtained by a Bruker Autoflex matrix assisted laser desorption/ionization-time of flight (MALDI-TOF) mass spectrometer (Bremen, Germany). C-dots was mixed with a 1.0M solution of DHB in MeOH/water (1:1v/v). Then 4.0µL of this mixture was deposited on a MALDI target plate and air-dried. The sample was irradiated by a pulsed N2 laser working at 337nm (3ns pulse, 3Hz). In general, 30 laser shots were averaged for each spectrum.

The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image of as-prepared C-dots was obtained with a JEOL JEM-1011 TEM (Tokyo, Japan) operating at 200kV. A droplet of C-dots solution was spotted onto Agar Scientific 400 mesh copper grids (Stansted, Essex, UK). Particle size was determined by the ImageJ 1.47 software (National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD, USA).

A stock standard solution of Kae (3.5mM) was prepared by dissolving 20mg Kae in 20mL MeOH. The stock solution was stored at 4°C until further use. Phosphate buffered solutions (PBS, 0.010M, pH 6.0) were prepared by dissolving 0.78g NaH2PO4·2H2O and 1.7907g Na2HPO4·12H2O in 500mL DDI water. PBS with different pHs were prepared by adjusting with a few drops of 1.0M NaOH or 1.0M HCl to the required pHs.

Fifteen tablets of xindakang were crushed and 1.50g of the powder was accurately weighed, dissolved in 10mL of MeOH, sonicated for 30min in the ultrasonic bath and centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 5min. The supernatant was collected and used for actual sample testing and the recovery experiment. Human blood samples were obtained from different healthy volunteers and stored at −20°C before use. For analysis, the samples were placed at room temperature for 3h and then centrifuged at 6000rpm for 15min to isolate the serum. The serum sample (1.00mL) was mixed with ACN (1.00mL) to precipitate proteins and then centrifuged at 8000rpm for 30min. The supernatant was collected and ACN was removed with a stream of N2 gas and kept at 4°C prior to analysis. For fluorescence measurements, 0.20mL of C-dots solution (1.5mg/mL) was mixed with 2.80mL of PBS (0.01M, pH 6.0) as the fluorescence probe. Then 20μL of xindakang or serum sample was added. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture solution was measured after 20min. Similarly, samples spiked with various concentrations of Kae were analyzed.

0.20mL of C-dots solution (1.5mg/mL) was added to 2.80mL PBS, followed by the addition of different amounts of Kae. The PL emission spectra (390–700nm) were recorded after 20min at an excitation wavelength of 370nm. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicates. All the fluorescence detections were under the same conditions. The slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 5nm. The fluorescence intensities F and F
o were recorded at excitation/emission of 370/490nm, where F and F
0 are the fluorescence intensities of C-dots in the presence and absence of Kae, respectively.

In quest of exploring the optical properties of the as-prepared C-dots, the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra are acquired. Fig. S2 displays the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared C-dots comprising an absorption peak at 215nm corresponding to the π→π ⁎ transition of the aromatic sp2 domain [44], a shoulder peak at 250nm originated from the formation of multiple polyaromatic chromophores [45–47] and a small shoulder peak at 300nm attributed to the n→π ⁎ transitions of C=O [47,48]. The C-dots sample depicts a strong emission peak at 490nm and an extra shoulder emission peak at 556nm upon excitation at 370nm, displaying strong yellowish green under a hand-held UV lamp (Inset of Fig. S2). The Φ
S of the as-prepared C-dots is 3.53% (Fig. S3). Fig. S4A depicts the PL spectra of the as-prepared C-dots in MeOH under various excitation wavelengths (λex). When excited at 350–460nm, especially at 360-410nm, the C-dots show strong green emission. However, with further increasing of the λex, the emission is red-shifted and the intensity decreases. Fig. S4B displays the PL spectra of the as-prepared C-dots in 1:14v/v MeOH/water sensing system. The PL behavior is similar to that of C-dots in MeOH.

IR spectroscopy is a useful technique to characterize the surface functionality of C-dots. Fig. S5 depicts the IR spectrum of the as-prepared C-dots, showing a broad absorption band around 3436cm-1 corresponding to the OH stretching of the surface-attached carboxylic acid group. The peaks at 2929 and 2859cm−1 correspond to the C–H stretching vibrations. The C-dots exhibits other characteristic absorption bands at 1662, 1624 and 1384cm−1, corresponding to the C=O, C=C stretching vibrations, and –CH3 umbrella bend, respectively [32]. In addition, the peaks at 2397 and 2302cm−1 correspond to P–OH bending of phosphate, indicating that C-dots contain surface-attached phosphate moiety. Peaks at 1160, 1085 and 960cm−1 are related to the vibration of P=O, P–O–C and P–O–H, respectively [44]. In summary, the as-prepared C-dots comprises sp3 (C–C), sp2 (C=C), sp2 (C=O) carbon atoms, and phosphocarbonaceous moieties.

To gain further insight into the surface functional group and element states of the as-prepared C-dots, XPS were acquired. Fig. S6A depicts the survey scan of the C-dots. Three peaks centered at 287, 534 and 135eV are associated with C1s, O1s and P2p, inferring the presence of C, O and P in the C-dots. Fig. S6B and C display the C1s and O1s XPS spectra of C-dots, respectively. The C1s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6, 286.3, 288.5, and 290.2eV corresponding to C=C, C–OH, C–O–C, and O=C–OH, respectively [49–51]. The O1s XPS spectrum can be fitted into three peaks at 532.2, 533.7 and 535.2eV associated with C=O, C–O–C and O=C–OH, respectively [50,52,53]. Fig. S6D reveals the P2p XPS spectra of C-dots. A deconvoluted P2p XPS spectrum shows a peak at 135.2eV for PO4 3−. In summary, the XPS data show the presence of C=C, C–O, C=O, C–OH, COOH, and PO4 3- surface-functionalities on the C-dots.

TEM has been traditionally used to obtain size information about C-dots. Fig. 1 A and B display a typical TEM image of the as-prepared C-dots and size distribution by counting randomly 200 particles from various spots of the sample during TEM image. The C-dots sample has a size range of 1.5–3.8nm and an average diameter of 2.6±0.3nm. The as-prepared C-dots are uniform in size and possess a nearly spherical shape.

MS is a valuable tool in identifying the mass of C-dots. Fig. 2 displays the MALDI-TOF MS of the as-prepared C-dots sample. The largest mass ion at m/z 3034 (tag with red asterisk) in the MS approximately indicate the relative molecular mass of the C-dots. Although it is difficult to deduce the exact chemical structure of the C-dots from its MS, the expanded MS spectra definitely give insights into the surface-attached functionalities of C-dots by examining their fragmentation patterns [54,55]. When the MS is expanded at the mass ranges 1000–2500, 800–1100 and 1700–2000 Da (Fig. S7). It is observed that the MS peaks show a serious of major m/z spacing in the mass units of 12, 15, 17, 18, 31, 43, 45, 112, 172, and 284 corresponding to the loss of C, CH3, OH, H2O, P, CH3CO, COOH, CH5PO4, C3H9PO6, and C4H14P2O10 fragments, respectively. These MS data indicate the presence of PO4 3-, COOH and CH3CO surface-functionalities on the C-dots and help us comprehend the chemical composition of the as-prepared C-dots which concurs with the IR and XPS data (vide supra).

The fluorescence of C-dots was found to be dependent on its concentration. Thus to adopt the C-dots for sensing, the baseline fluorescence intensity needs to be set to ensure that maximum analytical window is available for the later analyte detection. The effect of C-dots concentration on the fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. S8. The fluorescence intensity increases with the increase in concentration and reaches the highest at 0.10mg/mL and then drops with further increase in the concentration of C-dots. The decrease in fluorescence intensity at higher concentration is probably attributed to the common self-quenching effect. Thus, 0.10mg/mL C-dots was chosen for detection of Kae.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and reaction time
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of pH (2.0-12.0) on the fluorescence intensity of sensor system was studied by monitoring the fluorescence intensity as a function of pH in the presence and absence of Kae. Fig. 3A(1) depicts the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots without Kae. The intensity increases with the increase in pH until it reaches the highest at pH 6.0 and then decreases with further increase in pH. This indicates that the fluorescence intensity of C-dos is dependent on pH. Such observations are similar to those of C-dots modified with hydroxyl and carboxylic moieties [56,57]. Fig. 3 A(2) displays the effect of pH on the fluorescence of C-dots with 10.5µM Kae. Only small changes in intensity are observed when pH is varied. The intensity increases slightly with the increase in pH until it reaches the highest at pH 6.0 and then decreases slightly with further increase in pH. To find the optimum pH value, the relative fluorescence intensity (F
o /F) at different pHs is shown in Fig. 3B, where F
o and F are the fluorescence intensities of C-dots in the absence and presence of Kae, respectively. At pH 6.0, F
o /F is the largest; as a result, 6.0 was chosen as the optimal pH for this work.

Fig. S9 shows the change in fluorescence intensity of C-dots after adding Kae. The intensity drops dramatically within a minute and then steadily decreases until it reaches 20min. Further increase in reaction time produces a very drop in intensity. As such, 20min was chosen as the optimal reaction time for this work.

Excellent selectivity is important and necessary for a robust practical method. To explore the selectivity of the method using C-dots as the fluorescence probe for determination of Kae, the influences of potential interfering substances such as relevant metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Al3+, and Pb2+), amino acid (threonine, glycine, glutamic acid, alanine, histidine, cysteine, and tryptophan) and other coexisting substances (daidzein, ferulic acid, rutin, chrysophanol, chlorogenic acid, catechol, catechin, and l-epicatechin) which possibly existed in the real samples were investigated. Two experiments were performed. First, every interfering substance was studied as an independent sample to investigate the selectivity of the proposed method as depicted in Fig. 4 A. C-dots only exhibit significant response to Kae while the other interfering substances either show slight or negligible effect on C-dots. Second, Kae and other interfering substances were mixed together to form a mixture solution as a sample for selectivity testing as shown in Fig. 4B. Again, Kae could produce remarkable quenching effect on C-dots in the presence of the interfering substances. These results demonstrate that C-dots exhibit excellent selectivity to the detection of Kae. Although interfering substances such as rutin show some structural similarities to Kae, their chemical and steric structure, and space configuration are quite different. In fact, rutin contains an extra rutinoside (disaccharide rutinose-β-d-glucopyranose) unit while Kae does not. This could be the possible reason why C-dots has higher selectivity for Kae over rutin. However, the detailed reaction mechanisms of C-dots and Kae are still not clear.

Under the optimal conditions, the effect of Kae concentration on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots was studied and shown in Fig. 5 A. The fluorescence intensity of C-dots decreases significantly with the increase in Kae concentration, revealing that the sensing system is sensitive to Kae. The fluorescence quenching data follows the typical Stern–Volmer behavior [58,59]:
(1)
F
o
/ F = =
K
S V
[ C ]
+ 1
where F
o and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Kae, respectively, [C] is the concentration of Kae and KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. Fig. 5B displays the Stern–Volmer plot of F
o /F= 39,571[C]+1. A good linear relationship was observed at the concentration of Kae from 3.5 to 49µM with a correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9989. The limit of detection (LOD) was evaluated to be 38.4nM (S/N=3). To our knowledge, no fluorescence method for Kae based on the use of C-dots has been reported.

Table S1 compares the analytical performance of our developed method with some other reported methods for Kae determination [10,15,60–63]. The LOD of the proposed method is lower than other methods except the HPLC-chemiluminescence detection method. However, the developed method is simple, cost-effective and rapid. Furthermore, to assess the precision and accuracy of this method, determinations were carried out for a set of 10 measurements of 7, 21 and 35µM Kae under the optimal conditions. Table S2 shows the recovery test of Kae in the samples. The recovery is 97.9–102% and the RSD is 1.2–1.8%. These values demonstrate that our proposed method is accurate and precise.

The origin and quenching mechanism for inorganic fluorescent nanoparticals is yet to be fully understood, but there have been several suggestions from the literature such as inner filter effect, electron transfer process, non-radiative recombination pathways, Forster energy transfer, hole-trap and ion binding interaction [64–67]. The information of such mechanisms is more for those established quantum dots while not very detailed yet for C-dots. However, both are assumed to be of high similarity since the physiochemical properties are derived from the size effect that is due to the energy confinement below the size of the Bohr radius. Fig. 5 A indicates that Kae greatly quenches the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots as well as induce a hypsochromic shift of the emission spectrum (from 490 to 478nm) with the increase in the concentration of Kae. Since the surface of C-dots contains a lot of phosphocarbonaceous moieties, it is possible that Kae binds strongly with these functionalities of C-dots to induce the surface change of C-dots [68]. C-dots are known to be excellent electron acceptors and donors [69]. Thus, it is possible to facilitate an electron transfer from the photo-excited C-dots to the aromatic groups in Kae. In other words, C-dots are wrapped by Kae, allowing an effective non-radiative energy transfer from C-dots to Kae with a concomitant effect on quenching of C-dots [41].

The proposed sensing strategy was further tested with xindakang tablets and human serum samples to demonstrate its applicability. To detect Kae in xindakang tablets and human serum samples, standard addition method was used according to the general procedure with five replicates. Table 1 summarizes the analysis and the recovery test of Kae in xindakang tablets and blood serum samples. The recovery is 94.6–109% and 95.7–104% for xindakang tablets and human serum samples, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSD, n=5) are all less than 1.73%. The results indicate that the proposed sensing strategy has good precision and satisfactory repeatability. The proposed sensing system offers the potential for quantitative determination of Kae in real samples.

In summary, a simple, sensitive and cost-effective method has been developed for determination of Kae based on the change of fluorescence intensity of C-dots. The preparation of C-dots is simple and inexpensive. Compared to some other reported works for Kae, this fluorescent sensing strategy offers a couple of advantages. First, this method can be operated without expensive instrument which simplifies operations and reduces the associated costs. Second, the sensor exhibits good sensitivity and excellent selectivity. Furthermore, the proposed method could be employed to detect Kae in xindakang tablets and human serum with satisfactory results. It is anticipated that the present strategy is suitable for Kae detection in real samples. Finally, this work not only provides a new fluorescence quenching method for the detection of Kae but also enriches the application of C-dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21175085 and 21375083) and HKBU Faculty Research Grant (FRG1/13-14/039) are gratefully acknowledged. We would express our sincere thanks to Ms Winnie Y.K. Wu of the Institute of Advanced Materials for taking the TEM image and Ms Silva T. Mo of the Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University for acquiring the MALDI-TOF MS. The TEM was supported by the Special Equipment Grant from the University Grants Committee of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, China (Grant SEG_HKBU06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2015.07.004.
